examples.japanese_sentence: {漢字|かんじ}を100{個|こ}{覚|おぼ}えるつもりです。
examples.english_translation: I intend to memorize 100 Kanji.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～つもりです (tsumori desu) expresses a person's intention or plan to do something.

examples.japanese_sentence: セリフを{覚|おぼ}えてください。
examples.english_translation: Please memorize your lines.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～てください (te kudasai) is used to make a polite request.

examples.japanese_sentence: {宿題|しゅくだい}を{忘|わす}れないでください。
examples.english_translation: Please don't forget your homework.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～ないでください (nai de kudasai) is used to politely request that someone does not do an action.

examples.japanese_sentence: {傘|かさ}を{電車|でんしゃ}に{忘|わす}れました。
examples.english_translation: I forgot my umbrella on the train.
examples.grammar_explanation: The past tense (~mashita) indicates a completed action in the past.

examples.japanese_sentence: {財布|さいふ}をなくしてしまいました。
examples.english_translation: I've accidentally lost my wallet.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～てしまう (te shimau) indicates an action that was completed accidentally or with a sense of regret.

examples.japanese_sentence: パスポートをなくすと{大変|たいへん}ですよ。
examples.english_translation: It will be serious if you lose your passport.
examples.grammar_explanation: The conditional ～と (to) expresses a natural or certain consequence.

examples.japanese_sentence: {明日|あした}までにレポートを{出|だ}さなければなりません。
examples.english_translation: I must hand in the report by tomorrow.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～なければなりません (nakereba narimasen) expresses obligation or necessity.

examples.japanese_sentence: もうレポートを{出|だ}した？
examples.english_translation: Did you hand in the report already?
examples.grammar_explanation: Casual past tense (ta-form) used as a question with rising intonation.

examples.japanese_sentence: {現金|げんきん}で{払|はら}ってもいいですか。
examples.english_translation: Is it okay if I pay with cash?
examples.grammar_explanation: ～てもいいです (te mo ii desu) is used to ask for permission.

examples.japanese_sentence: {明日|あした}までにお{金|かね}を{払|はら}わなくてもいいです。
examples.english_translation: You don't have to pay the money by tomorrow.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～なくてもいいです (nakutemo ii desu) indicates that an action is not necessary.

examples.japanese_sentence: {図書館|としょかん}に{本|ほん}を{返|かえ}します。
examples.english_translation: I will return the book to the library.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle 'ni' indicates the destination or recipient of the action.

examples.japanese_sentence: {返|かえ}してほしいです。
examples.english_translation: I want you to return it.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～てほしい (te hoshii) is used to express a desire for someone else to do something.

examples.japanese_sentence: これから{出|で}かけます。
examples.english_translation: I am going out now.
examples.grammar_explanation: The non-past form (masu) can indicate a future action about to take place.

examples.japanese_sentence: {父|ちち}は{出|で}かけています。
examples.english_translation: My father is out (has gone out).
examples.grammar_explanation: ～ている (te iru) can describe a state resulting from a previous action.

examples.japanese_sentence: ここで{靴|くつ}を{脱|ぬ}いでください。
examples.english_translation: Please take off your shoes here.
examples.grammar_explanation: The te-form + kudasai is used for making a standard polite request.

examples.japanese_sentence: コートを{脱|ぬ}いでもいい？
examples.english_translation: Can I take off my coat?
examples.grammar_explanation: Casual form of asking for permission (te-form + mo ii).

examples.japanese_sentence: {弁当|べんとう}を{持|も}って{行|い}きます。
examples.english_translation: I will take my lunch box with me.
examples.grammar_explanation: Compound verb 'motte' (holding) + 'iku' (going) showing movement away from the speaker.

examples.japanese_sentence: {何|なに}も{持|も}って{行|い}かないでください。
examples.english_translation: Please don't take anything with you.
examples.grammar_explanation: The 'nai' form + 'de kudasai' for a negative request.

examples.japanese_sentence: {明日|あした}、{辞書|じしょ}を{持|も}って{来|く}てください。
examples.english_translation: Please bring a dictionary tomorrow.
examples.grammar_explanation: Compound verb 'motte' (holding) + 'kuru' (coming) showing movement toward the speaker.

examples.japanese_sentence: {飲|の}み{物|もの}を{持|も}って{来|く}たほうがいいですよ。
examples.english_translation: It's better to bring a drink.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～たほうがいい (ta hou ga ii) is used to give advice.

examples.japanese_sentence: {心配|しんぱい}しないでください。
examples.english_translation: Please don't worry.
examples.grammar_explanation: Negative request using the 'nai' form of the suru-verb.

examples.japanese_sentence: {両親|りょうしん}が{心配|しんぱい}しています。
examples.english_translation: My parents are worried.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～ている (te iru) describes a current continuous state or emotion.

examples.japanese_sentence: {今日|きょう}は{残業|ざんぎょう}したくありません。
examples.english_translation: I don't want to work overtime today.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～たい (tai) expresses desire; its negative form is ～たくない (takunai).

examples.japanese_sentence: {昨日|きのう}は10{時|じ}まで{残業|ざんぎょう}しました。
examples.english_translation: I worked overtime until 10 o'clock yesterday.
examples.grammar_explanation: Past tense of a suru-verb in the polite form.

examples.japanese_sentence: {来月|らいげつ}アメリカへ{出張|しゅっちょう}します。
examples.english_translation: I will go on a business trip to America next month.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle 'he' (e) indicates the direction/destination.

examples.japanese_sentence: {出張|しゅっちょう}しなければなりませんか。
examples.english_translation: Do I have to go on a business trip?
examples.grammar_explanation: Question form of obligation (~nakereba narimasen ka).

examples.japanese_sentence: {食後|しょくご}に{薬|くすり}を{飲|の}んでください。
examples.english_translation: Please take the medicine after meals.
examples.grammar_explanation: In Japanese, medicine is 'drunk' (nomu) rather than 'eaten' (taberu).

examples.japanese_sentence: {薬|くすり}を{飲|の}んだほうがいいです。
examples.english_translation: You should take your medicine.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～たほうがいい (ta hou ga ii) for giving strong advice.

examples.japanese_sentence: お{風呂|ふろ}に{入|はい}ります。
examples.english_translation: I will take a bath.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle 'ni' is used with 'hairu' to indicate entering/going into the bath.

examples.japanese_sentence: お{風呂|ふろ}に{入|はい}りたいです。
examples.english_translation: I want to take a bath.
examples.grammar_explanation: The tai-form added to the verb stem expresses the speaker's desire.

examples.japanese_sentence: これは{大切|たいせつ}な{書類|しょるい}です。
examples.english_translation: This is an important document.
examples.grammar_explanation: 'Taisetsu' is a na-adjective; it uses 'na' when modifying a noun.

examples.japanese_sentence: {家族|かぞく}を{大切|たいせつ}にします。
examples.english_translation: I will value/cherish my family.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～を大切にする (o taisetsu ni suru) is an idiom meaning to cherish or take care of something.

examples.japanese_sentence: {大丈夫|だいじょうぶ}ですか。
examples.english_translation: Are you okay?
examples.grammar_explanation: A standard phrase used to check on someone's well-being.

examples.japanese_sentence: いいえ、{大丈夫|だいじょうぶ}じゃありません。
examples.english_translation: No, it's not okay.
examples.grammar_explanation: 'Ja arimasen' is the formal negative form of the copula used with na-adjectives.

examples.japanese_sentence: {危|あぶ}ない！{気|き}をつけて！
examples.english_translation: Watch out! Be careful!
examples.grammar_explanation: Casual exclamation using the dictionary form of the i-adjective.

examples.japanese_sentence: ここは{危|あぶ}なくないです。
examples.english_translation: This place is not dangerous.
examples.grammar_explanation: To make an i-adjective negative, replace 'i' with 'kunai'.

examples.japanese_sentence: この{問題|もんだい}は{難|むずか}しいです。
examples.english_translation: This problem/question is difficult.
examples.grammar_explanation: 'Kono' is a pre-noun adjectival that points to an object near the speaker.

examples.japanese_sentence: {何|なに}か{問題|もんだい}がありますか。
examples.english_translation: Is there some kind of problem?
examples.grammar_explanation: The existence verb 'arimasu' is used for inanimate objects like 'problems'.

examples.japanese_sentence: {正|ただ}しい{答え|こたえ}を{選|えら}んでください。
examples.english_translation: Please choose the correct answer.
examples.grammar_explanation: The direct object of the verb is marked by the particle 'o'.

examples.japanese_sentence: {答え|こたえ}が{分|わ}かりません。
examples.english_translation: I don't know the answer.
examples.grammar_explanation: The verb 'wakarimasu' (to understand/know) usually takes the particle 'ga' for its object.

examples.japanese_sentence: この{部屋|へや}は{禁煙|きんえん}です。
examples.english_translation: This room is non-smoking.
examples.grammar_explanation: Noun + desu (copula) to state a fact/rule.

examples.japanese_sentence: {禁煙|きんえん}しましょう。
examples.english_translation: Let's quit smoking / Let's make it non-smoking.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～ましょう (mashou) is the volitional form used for making a suggestion or invitation.

vocabulary.reading_furigana: けんこうほけんしょう

examples.japanese_sentence: {健康保険証|けんこうほけんしょう}を{持|も}っていますか。
examples.english_translation: Do you have your health insurance card?
examples.grammar_explanation: ～ている (te iru) shows a state of possession with the verb 'motsu'.

examples.japanese_sentence: {健康保険証|けんこうほけんしょう}を{忘|わす}れないで。
examples.english_translation: Don't forget your health insurance card.
examples.grammar_explanation: Casual negative request (nai-form + de).

examples.japanese_sentence: {風邪|かぜ}をひきました。
examples.english_translation: I caught a cold.
examples.grammar_explanation: 'Kaze o hiku' is the standard verb phrase for catching a cold.

examples.japanese_sentence: {風邪|かぜ}かもしれない。
examples.english_translation: It might be a cold.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～かもしれない (ka mo shirenai) expresses possibility or conjecture.

examples.japanese_sentence: {熱|ねつ}があります。
examples.english_translation: I have a fever.
examples.grammar_explanation: The existence verb 'arimasu' is used to state that you have a symptom.

examples.japanese_sentence: {熱|ねつ}がなかなか{下|さ}がりません。
examples.english_translation: The fever just won't go down.
examples.grammar_explanation: 'Nakanaka' with a negative verb indicates that something doesn't happen easily.

examples.japanese_sentence: {病気|びょうき}になりました。
examples.english_translation: I became ill.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～になる (ni naru) indicates a change of state.

examples.japanese_sentence: {父|ちち}は{病気|びょうき}で{入院|にゅういん}しています。
examples.english_translation: My father is in the hospital due to illness.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle 'de' here indicates the reason or cause of the hospitalization.

examples.japanese_sentence: この{薬|くすり}はよく{効|き}きます。
examples.english_translation: This medicine works well.
examples.grammar_explanation: 'Kiku' is the verb used to describe the effectiveness of medicine.

examples.japanese_sentence: {薬|くすり}を{飲|の}みたくないです。
examples.english_translation: I don't want to take medicine.
examples.grammar_explanation: The negative desire form (stem + takunai desu).

examples.japanese_sentence: お{風呂|ふろ}が{沸|わ}きました。
examples.english_translation: The bath is ready (boiled).
examples.grammar_explanation: The verb 'wakimasu' refers to water reaching the desired temperature for a bath.

examples.japanese_sentence: ゆっくりお{風呂|ふろ}に{入|はい}ってください。
examples.english_translation: Please take a long, relaxing bath.
examples.grammar_explanation: The adverb 'yukkuri' (slowly/at ease) modifies the action of taking a bath.

examples.japanese_sentence: {上着|うわぎ}を{脱|ぬ}いでもいいですよ。
examples.english_translation: You may take off your jacket.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～てもいいです (te mo ii desu) is used here to grant permission.

examples.japanese_sentence: {寒|さむ}いから{上着|うわぎ}を{着|き}なさい。
examples.english_translation: Wear your jacket because it's cold.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～なさい (nasai) is a commanding form often used by parents or teachers.

examples.japanese_sentence: {下着|したぎ}を{買|か}わなければなりません。
examples.english_translation: I must buy some underwear.
examples.grammar_explanation: Obligation form using 'nakereba narimasen'.

examples.japanese_sentence: {下着|したぎ}を{洗|あら}った？
examples.english_translation: Did you wash the underwear?
examples.grammar_explanation: Casual question using the past tense (ta-form).

examples.japanese_sentence: {先生|せんせい}に{診|み}てもらいましょう。
examples.english_translation: Let's have the doctor check you.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～てもらう (te morau) implies receiving an action from someone; 'mashou' makes it a suggestion.

examples.japanese_sentence: {先生|せんせい}の{話|はなし}を{聞|き}きました。
examples.english_translation: I listened to the doctor's talk.
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle 'no' shows possession or relationship between the doctor and the talk.

examples.japanese_sentence: {二|に}、{三日|さんにち}{休|やす}んでください。
examples.english_translation: Please rest for a few days.
examples.grammar_explanation: Used as an adverbial phrase to indicate duration.

examples.japanese_sentence: {二|に}、{三日|さんにち}で{治|なお}るでしょう。
examples.english_translation: It will probably get better in a few days.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～でしょう (deshou) expresses probability or conjecture.

examples.japanese_sentence: {二|に}、{三枚|さんまい}{紙|かみ}をください。
examples.english_translation: Please give me a few sheets of paper.
examples.grammar_explanation: 'Mai' is a counter for thin, flat objects like paper.

examples.japanese_sentence: {二|に}、{三回|さんかい}{読|よ}みました。
examples.english_translation: I read it a few times.
examples.grammar_explanation: 'Kai' is a counter for the number of times an action occurs.

examples.japanese_sentence: 12{時|じ}までに{帰|かえ}ります。
examples.english_translation: I will return by 12 o'clock.
examples.grammar_explanation: ～までに (made ni) indicates a time limit before which an action must be completed.

examples.japanese_sentence: {土曜日|どようび}までに{書|か}いてください。
examples.english_translation: Please write it by Saturday.
examples.grammar_explanation: Used with days of the week to set a deadline.

examples.japanese_sentence: {雨|あめ}です。ですから、どこへも{行|い}きません。
examples.english_translation: It's raining. Therefore, I'm not going anywhere.
examples.grammar_explanation: 'Desu kara' is a conjunction used at the start of a sentence to provide a reason for what follows.

examples.japanese_sentence: {危|あぶ}ないですよ。ですから、{触|さわ}らないでください。
examples.english_translation: It's dangerous. So, please don't touch it.
examples.grammar_explanation: Connects the danger of a situation to a prohibitive request.

examples.japanese_sentence: {顔色|かおいろ}が{悪|わる}いですね。どうしましたか。
examples.english_translation: You look pale. What's the matter?
examples.grammar_explanation: A polite phrase used by doctors or friends to ask about someone's condition.

examples.japanese_sentence: どうしたの？{泣|な}かないで。
examples.english_translation: What's wrong? Don't cry.
examples.grammar_explanation: 'Dou shita no' is the casual version of 'dou shimashita ka'.

examples.japanese_sentence: {頭|あたま}が{痛|いた}いです。
examples.english_translation: I have a headache (My head is painful).
examples.grammar_explanation: The particle 'ga' marks the subject (the body part) that is painful.

examples.japanese_sentence: どこが{痛|いた}いですか。
examples.english_translation: Where does it hurt?
examples.grammar_explanation: Question form using the interrogative 'doko'.

examples.japanese_sentence: のどが{痛|いた}いです。
examples.english_translation: I have a sore throat.
examples.grammar_explanation: Standard structure for expressing pain in a specific body part.

examples.japanese_sentence: のどが{乾|かわ}きました。
examples.english_translation: I'm thirsty (My throat dried).
examples.grammar_explanation: Past tense of 'kawaku' (to dry) is used to express being currently thirsty.

vocabulary.meaning_english: Take care of yourself. Get well soon.

examples.japanese_sentence: お{先|さき}に{失礼|しつれい}します。お{大事|だいじ}に。
examples.english_translation: I'll be leaving now. Take care of yourself.
examples.grammar_explanation: A set phrase used when parting from someone who is ill.

examples.japanese_sentence: 無理しないでくださいね。お大事に。
examples.english_translation: Please don't overdo it. Get well soon.
examples.grammar_explanation: Often paired with advice to rest or not work too hard.
